elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we reinterpret quadratic Lyapunov functions as solutions to a performance estimation saddle point problem.
elements.sent-2411-002: This allows us to automatically detect the existence of such a Lyapunov function and thus numerically check that a given algorithm converges.
elements.sent-2411-003: The novelty of this work is that we show how to define the saddle point problem using the PEPit software and then solve it with DSP-CVXPY.
elements.sent-2411-004: This combination gives us a very strong modeling power because defining new points and their relations across iterates is very easy in PEPit.
elements.sent-2411-005: We can without effort define auxiliary points used for the sole purpose of designing more complex Lyapunov functions, define complex functional classes like the class of convex-concave saddle point problems whose smoothed duality gap has the quadratic error bound property or study complex algorithms like primal-dual coordinate descent method.
elements.sent-2411-006: Performance estimation problems (PEP), introduced in \cite{drori2014performance , are optimization problems whose goal is to study the worst case performance of optimization algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-007: When combined with interpolation results on the functional class of interest, they can be used to show tighter bounds and design new, faster algorithms~\cite{taylor2017convex .}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the optimization algorithms that we get with PEP have a number of variables and constraints that grow linearly with the number of iterations we wish to consider.
elements.sent-2411-009: Since they are eventually solved using semi-definite programming solvers, this can be quickly a challenging task.
elements.sent-2411-010: In order to study the convergence of complex algorithms, a widely used technique is to exhibit a Lyapunov function, which is a nonnegative function whose value is strictly decreasing at each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-011: It has been shown in~\cite{upadhyaya2024automated that performance estimation problems can be used to automatically detect the presence of a quadratic Lyapunov function and thus numerically check the convergence of an algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This was used for instance to show that the Chambolle-Pock algorithm converges for a wider range of step sizes than previously known~\cite{banert2023chambolle .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this paper, we reinterpret quadratic Lyapunov functions as the solutions to a performance estimation saddle point problem.
elements.sent-2411-014: Like in \cite{upadhyaya2024automated , this allows us to automatically detect the existence of such a Lyapunov function and thus numerically check that a given algorithm converges.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The novelty of this work is that we show how to define the saddle point problem using the PEPit software \cite{goujaud2024pepit and then solve it with DSP-CVXPY \cite{schiele2023disciplined .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We conducted three numerical experiments to show the interest of our contribution.
elements.sent-2411-017: Firstly, we show that we can define complex functional classes and analyze the worst case behavior of algorithms on this class.
elements.sent-2411-018: We considered the class of convex-concave saddle point problems whose smoothed duality gap has the quadratic error bound property \cite{fercoq2023quadratic and showed that the primal-dual hybrid gradient method~\cite{chambolle2011first converges linearly under this assumption.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: More precisely, our study suggests that the fastest rate is not attained with the largest step sizes.
elements.sent-2411-020: Secondly, we show that we can numerically study a randomized algorithm, namely randomized coordinate gradient descent~\cite{nesterov2012efficiency .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Indeed, if we try to estimate all possible outcomes of the algorithm, we quickly face an explosion in the number of possibilities.
elements.sent-2411-022: Thanks to this study, we could find a slightly improved worst case bound on the expected objective value.
elements.sent-2411-023: Finally, we show that we can study complex algorithms like the primal-dual coordinate descent method \cite{alacaoglu2020random .}
elements.sent-2411-024: In that case, we reached the limits of the approach, with solvers struggling to return accurate solutions and the solutions returned difficult to interpret.. \revisionone{.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we are going to study the convergence of optimization algorithms using Lyapunov functions.
elements.sent-2411-026: We shall consider a class of functions $\mathcal F$ satisfying some properties like convexity or smoothness.
elements.sent-2411-027: A function $f \in \mathcal F$ will be real valued $f: \mathcal X \to \mathbb R$ and we will consider a list of points $x = (\xi_i)_{1 \leq i \leq p \in \mathcal X^p$ on which an algorithm $A$ may act.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Those $p$ points, linked to the concept of leaf points in PEPit \cite{goujaud2024pepit , can be considered as intermediate points in the construction of the next iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We will assume that they are not linearly dependent but they may depend one on the other with relation that involve the function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Then we will denote $A(f, x) \in \mathcal X^p$ the next iterate, which is the transformation of each leaf point $\xi_i$ into a new vector of $\mathcal X$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Of course the way each iterations proceeds depends on the function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, to encode the fixed-step gradient descent algorithm on the class $\mathcal F_{0,L}$ of convex functions with $L$-Lipschitz gradient, we shall take $\xi_1 \in \mathcal X$, $\xi_2 = \nabla f(\xi_1)$, $\xi_3 \in \arg\min f$ and \begin{equation}A(f, [\xi_1, \xi_2, \xi_3]) = [\xi_1 - \gamma \xi_2, \nabla f(\xi_1 - \gamma \xi_2),\xi_3] \;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: By an abuse of notation, for a matrix $Q \in \mathbb R^{p \times p}$ and a list of vectors $x \in \mathcal X^p$, we shall denote $x^\top Q x = \sum_{i=1 ^p\sum_{j=1 ^p Q_{i,j \langle x_i, x_j\rangle$.}}}
elements.section-2411-003: From Lyapunov function to saddle point problem
elements.sent-2411-034: A widely used technique to prove convergence of an algorithm is to find a Lyapunov function $V \geq 0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_lyap} V(f, A(f, x)) + R(f, x) \leq V(f, x) \qquad \forall f \in \mathcal F, \forall x \in \mathcal X\end{equation} where $A(f,x)$ is the outcome of one iteration of algorithm $A$ applied on function $f$ at the point $x$.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_lyap} V(f, A(f, x)) + R(f, x) \leq V(f, x) \qquad \forall f \in \mathcal F, \forall x \in \mathcal X\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: In order to prove linear convergence, we are looking for a real number $0 < \rho < 1$ and a function $V$ such that \begin{equation}R(f, A(f,x)) \leq V(f, A(f, x)) \leq \rho V(f, x) \qquad \forall x \in \mathcal X, \forall f \in \mathcal F\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}R(f, A(f,x)) \leq V(f, A(f, x)) \leq \rho V(f, x) \qquad \forall x \in \mathcal X, \forall f \in \mathcal F\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-036: Consider the gradient descent method on the class of differentiable convex functions whose gradient is $L$-Lipschitz continuous.. We can choose:.
elements.sent-2411-037: If we are able to find a Lyapunov function $V$ then sublinear convergence of the function value gap will follow by standard arguments~\cite{nesterov2013introductory .}
elements.sent-2411-038: This can also be used to get finite-time convergence rates.
elements.sent-2411-039: Let us now present the main derivation of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-040: We shall consider the set $\mathcal Q$ of quadratic Lyapunov functions parameterized as \begin{equation}V(f, x) = x^\top Q x + q^\top N(f, x)\end{equation} for some positive semi-definite matrix $Q \in \mathbb S^p_+$, some nonnegative vector $q$, some minimizer $x^*$ of $f$ and nonnegative quantities $N(f, x)$ that depend on the problem only.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}V(f, x) = x^\top Q x + q^\top N(f, x)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: But the inner maximization problem fits into the framework of performance estimation problems \cite{drori2014performance,taylor2017convex .}
elements.section-2411-004: Constructing the transition matrix using PEPit
elements.sent-2411-042: Suppose that one iteration of the algorithm can be described using $\bar p$ leaf points $(\xi_1, \ldots, \xi_{\bar p )$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Then the matrix $G$ is called the Gram matrix and is such that $G_{i,j = \langle \xi_i, \xi_j\rangle$ for all $j \in \{1, \ldots, \bar p\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The change of variable from $x = (\xi_1, \ldots, \xi_{\bar p )$ to $G$ is surjective as soon as $\dim(\mathcal X) \geq \bar p$, as can be seen thanks to a Cholesky factorization.}
elements.sent-2411-045: With this matrix, we can rewrite $x^\top Q x = \sum_{i,j Q_{i,j \langle \xi_i, \xi_j\rangle = \tr(Q G)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Let us now consider a vector $y \in \mathcal X$.
elements.sent-2411-047: When defined in PEPit, any such vector is either a new leaf point or a linear combination of previously defined leaf point.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the latter case, PEPit stores the leaf point representation of $y$.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this paper, we shall denote $\pi(y)$ this leaf point representation, which means that $y = \sum_{k=1 ^{\bar p \pi(y)_k \xi_k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In particular, $\pi(\xi_i) = e_i$, the $i$th canonical basis vector.
elements.sent-2411-051: We now define the matrix $\Sigma$, which we shall call the transition matrix, by \begin{equation}\Sigma \pi(\xi_i) = \pi((A(f, (\xi_j)_{1 \leq j \leq p}))_i) \;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: In~\cite{upadhyaya2024automated , a similar transition matrix is defined.}
elements.sent-2411-053: More precisely it is the transpose of what we present here.
elements.sent-2411-054: Let us have a look at how the Gram matrix is transformed when one iteration takes place: \begin{equation}G^+_{i,j} = \langle (A(f,x))_i, (A(f, x))_j\rangle = \sum_{k=1}^{\bar p} \sum_{l=1}^{\bar p} \pi(A(f,x))_i)_k\pi((A(f, x))_j)_l\langle \xi_k, \xi_l \rangle = \sum_{k=1}^{\bar p} \sum_{l=1}^{\bar p} \Sigma_{k,i} \Sigma_{l,j}G_{k,l} G^+ = \Sigma^\top G \Sigma\end{equation} Finally, we get \begin{equation}A(f,x)^\top Q A(f,x) = \sum_{i,j} Q_{i,j} \langle (A(f,x))_i, (A(f, x))_j\rangle = \sum_{i,j} Q_{i,j} G_{i,j}^+ = \tr(Q \Sigma^\top G \Sigma) \;.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}G^+_{i,j} = \langle (A(f,x))_i, (A(f, x))_j\rangle = \sum_{k=1}^{\bar p} \sum_{l=1}^{\bar p} \pi(A(f,x))_i)_k\pi((A(f, x))_j)_l\langle \xi_k, \xi_l \rangle = \sum_{k=1}^{\bar p} \sum_{l=1}^{\bar p} \Sigma_{k,i} \Sigma_{l,j}G_{k,l} G^+ = \Sigma^\top G \Sigma\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}A(f,x)^\top Q A(f,x) = \sum_{i,j} Q_{i,j} \langle (A(f,x))_i, (A(f, x))_j\rangle = \sum_{i,j} Q_{i,j} G_{i,j}^+ = \tr(Q \Sigma^\top G \Sigma) \;.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: Using similar ideas, we can define a transition matrix $\sigma$ for leaf expressions, such that if $N(f, x) = N F$ then $N(f, A(f,x)) = N \sigma F$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Gathering these we can write \eqref{lyap-minmax as a saddle point problem with semi-definite variables: \begin{equation}\exists V \in \mathcal Q  s.t.  V(A(f, x), x^*) + R(x, x^*) \leq V(x, x^*) \Leftrightarrow \quad \revisionone{\min_{\{Q \succeq 0, q \geq 0\}} \max \left\{ \tr(Q \Sigma^\top G \Sigma) + q^\top N \sigma F + c + \tr(C G) - \tr(Q G) - q^\top N F subject to:  a_i + b_i^\top F + \tr(D_iG) \leq 0, \quad \forall i \in I \right\} }\leq 0\end{equation} The constraints of the inner problem are positive semi-definiteness, linear inequality and linear equality constraints that are automatically built by the PEPit software~\cite{goujaud2024pepit .}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\exists V \in \mathcal Q  s.t.  V(A(f, x), x^*) + R(x, x^*) \leq V(x, x^*) \Leftrightarrow \quad \revisionone{\min_{\{Q \succeq 0, q \geq 0\}} \max \left\{ \tr(Q \Sigma^\top G \Sigma) + q^\top N \sigma F + c + \tr(C G) - \tr(Q G) - q^\top N F subject to:  a_i + b_i^\top F + \tr(D_iG) \leq 0, \quad \forall i \in I \right\} }\leq 0\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: We are left with a convex-concave saddle point problem which can be solved by the off-the-shelf solver DS-CVXPY~\cite{schiele2023disciplined .}
elements.sent-2411-058: We now show how PEPit can help us implement the transition matrix $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-059: When we define a new point in PEPit, two cases can occur.
elements.sent-2411-060: It is either a linear combination of previously defined points or it is what is called a leaf point.
elements.sent-2411-061: Defining a new leaf point means that a new variable is required in the performance estimation problem.
elements.sent-2411-062: It is then associated with constraints that will ensure that it truly represents what it is meant to represent.
elements.sent-2411-063: For instance, when defining the gradient descent method on a function $f$, we first define $x^* = 0$, where $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ and an initial point $x$, which will be a leaf point with constraint $\|x - x^*\|^2 \leq R$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Then, in order to represent $x^+= x - \gamma \nabla f(x)$, we need a second leaf point, which is $\nabla f(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: PEPit automatically defines all the constraints that are necessary to ensure that $\nabla f(x)$ is interpolable with a function of the class chosen for $f$.
elements.sent-2411-066: The new iterate $x^+$ is not a leaf point because it can be recovered as a linear combination of other points.
elements.sent-2411-067: In order to illustrate the construction of the matrix $\Sigma$, suppose $R(x) = \|\nabla f(x^+)\|^2$, so that we are also interested in $\nabla f(x^+)$.
elements.sent-2411-068:
The Gram matrix $G$ is such that  egin{equation}G =  x^	op x x^	op

abla f(x)^	op x 
abla f(x)^	op

abla f(x^+) 
abla f(x^+)^	op x 
abla f(x^+)^	op 
abla f(x) 
abla f(x^+)^	op 
abla f(x^+)  \; .\end{equation}.

egin{equation}G =  x^	op x x^	op

abla f(x^+)

elements.sent-2411-069: We take $p = 2$ (the number of leaf points we consider in our Lyapunov function) and we have $\bar p = 3$ (the number of leaf points constructed by PEPit to describe the algorithm).

Hence,  egin{equation}\Sigma =  1 0 0 -\gamma 0 0 0 1 0 \end{equation} We can compute  egin{equation}\Sigma^	op G \Sigma =  (x^+)^	op x^+ (x^+)^	op

elements.sent-2411-073: In order to construct $\Sigma$, we consider the leaf points where the Lyapunov function $V$ is going to be computed and their images after applying the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-074: Let $u$ be such a leaf point ($u \in \{x, \nabla f(x)\}$ for the gradient descent case).
elements.sent-2411-075: If we consider the decomposition dictionary of the product $u^\top u^+$, then we directly obtain the row of $\Sigma$ corresponding to $u$.
elements.sent-2411-076: This decomposition dictionary $W$ is a matrix of the same size as $G$ such that $\tr(W^\top G) = u^\top u^+$.
elements.sent-2411-077: It is implemented in PEPit, so directly usable.
elements.sent-2411-078: Note that we could also implement the matrix $\Sigma$ from the decomposition dictionary of $u^+$ in leaf points but the proposed solution allows one to directly manipulate matrices.
elements.sent-2411-079: We considered three numerical experiments that show how the technique of automatic Lyapunov functions may be used to better understand optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-080: The code for the numerical experiments is available on \href{https://perso.telecom-paris.fr/ofercoq/Software.html {https://perso.telecom-paris.fr/ofercoq/Software.html.}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Linear convergence of PDHG with longer steps under quadratic error bound of the smoothed gap
elements.sent-2411-081: Consider the Primal-Dual Hybrid Gradient (PDHG) algorithm designed to solve the saddle point problem \begin{equation}\min_{x\in \mathcal X} \max_{y \in \mathcal Y} f(x) + \langle Mx, y \rangle - g(y) \;.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\min_{x\in \mathcal X} \max_{y \in \mathcal Y} f(x) + \langle Mx, y \rangle - g(y) \;.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: PDHG is the algorithm \begin{equation}\bar y_{k+1} = \prox_{\sigma g}(y_k + \sigma M x_k) x_{k+1} = \bar x_{k+1} = \prox_{\tau f}(x_k - \tau M^\top \bar y_{k+1}) y_{k+1} = \bar y_{k+1} + \sigma M(\bar x_{k+1} - x_k)\end{equation} It has been shown in \cite{fercoq2023quadratic that PDHG converges linearly under the assumption that the smoothed duality gap has the quadratic error bound property.}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\bar y_{k+1} = \prox_{\sigma g}(y_k + \sigma M x_k) x_{k+1} = \bar x_{k+1} = \prox_{\tau f}(x_k - \tau M^\top \bar y_{k+1}) y_{k+1} = \bar y_{k+1} + \sigma M(\bar x_{k+1} - x_k)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-083: This proof of linear convergence requires $\gamma < 1$ where $\gamma = \sigma \tau \|M\|^2$, $\sigma$ is the dual step size, $\tau$ is the primal step size.
elements.sent-2411-084: Nevertheless, the algorithm does converge for $\gamma = \sigma \tau \|M\|^2 < 4/3$ \cite{li2022improved .}
elements.sent-2411-085: In the experiment summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:pdhg_qebsm_gamma , we numerically extend the analysis of \cite{fercoq2023quadratic to the full range $0 < \gamma < 4/3$ for the case $\tau = \gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\gamma {\|M\|}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Of course, the result may be subject to numerical instabilities and we do not have a closed formula for the rate, but it has been obtained without having to manipulate a complex combination of inequalities.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{pepit_pdhg_rate_qeb_sm} \caption{Rate of convergence of PDHG as a function of $\gamma$ ($1-\rho$ close to $10^0$ means very fast convergence). Each curve corresponds to a given value for the quadratic error bound constant for the smoothed gap with parameter $\beta=1$. Note that as shown in \cite{li2022improved}, $\gamma$ can be chosen up to 4/3. However, in terms of rate of convergence, this figure suggests not to try approaching too much the convergence frontier.} \label{fig:pdhg_qebsm_gamma}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Better Lyapunov function for coordinate descent
elements.sent-2411-087: In this experiment, we show how to use our results for a randomized algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-089: We consider a convex differentiable function $f$ whose $i$th partial derivative is $L_{i}$-Lipschitz continuous.
elements.sent-2411-090: Suppose we have $d$ blocks of coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-091: The algorithm is \begin{equation}Generate I_{k+1} \sim U(\{1, \ldots, d\}) x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{1}{L_{I_{k+1}}} \nabla_{I_{k+1}} f(x_k) U_{I_{k+1}}\end{equation} where $U_i$ is the submatrix of the identity corresponding to block $i$.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}Generate I_{k+1} \sim U(\{1, \ldots, d\}) x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{1}{L_{I_{k+1}}} \nabla_{I_{k+1}} f(x_k) U_{I_{k+1}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-092: In~\cite{lu2015complexity , it is shown that for all $k$, \begin{equation}\mathbb E[d(f(x_{k+1}) - f(x_*)) + \frac{d}{2} \|x_{k+1} - x_*\|^2_L | x_k] \leq (d-1)(f(x_k) - f(x_*)) + \frac{d}{2} \|x_{k} - x_*\|^2_L\end{equation} Our goal is to recover this result, and perhaps improve it.}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\mathbb E[d(f(x_{k+1}) - f(x_*)) + \frac{d}{2} \|x_{k+1} - x_*\|^2_L | x_k] \leq (d-1)(f(x_k) - f(x_*)) + \frac{d}{2} \|x_{k} - x_*\|^2_L\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: Since it is a randomized algorithm, we need to consider all the possible outcomes of the random process.
elements.sent-2411-094: Fortunately, the Lyapunov analysis amounts to studying only one iteration, from $x_0$ to $x_1$, so that there are only $d$ possible outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-095: We construct $d$ possible values for $x_{1 (I_{1 = i)$ and $d$ transition matrices $\Sigma(I_{1 = i)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The final Lyapunov function is then the average of all the possible outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-097: Using our automatic Lyapunov technique, we considered this algorithm for the case $L_{i = 1$ for all $i$ and various values for $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: We then obtain nonnegative numbers $q_1$ and $q_2$ such that for all $f$ satisfying the assumptions, \begin{equation}\mathbb E[q_1 (f(x_1) - f(x_*)) + q_2 \|x_1 - x_*\|^2_L] \leq (q_1-1) (f(x_0) - f(x_*)) + q_2 \|x_0 - x_*\|^2_L\end{equation} We also tried considering additional terms involving $\nabla f(x_0)$ but this did not improve the results.

elements.sent-2411-109: As a final experiment, we show that we can deal with a complex algorithm, namely PURE-CD~\cite{alacaoglu2020random , which is a primal-dual algorithm with randomized coordinate updates.}
elements.sent-2411-110: It has been designed to solve problems of the form \begin{equation}\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}_1 \times \ldots \times \mathcal X_n} \max_{y \in \mathcal Y} f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}g_i(x_i) + \langle Mx, y \rangle - h^*(y),\end{equation} $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ are Euclidean spaces such that $\mathcal{X = \prod_{i=1 ^n \mathcal{X _i$, and $\mathcal{Y =\prod_{j=1 ^m \mathcal{Y _j$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation}\min_{x\inX_1 \times \ldots \times \mathcal X_n} \max_{y \in \mathcal Y} f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}g_i(x_i) + \langle Mx, y \rangle - h^*(y),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-111: The functions $f\colon \mathcal{X \to \mathbb{R}$, $g_i \colon \mathcal X_i \to \mathbb R \cup \{+\infty\}$ and $h\colon \mathcal{Y \to \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ are proper, lower semicontinuous, and convex, $M\colon \mathcal{X \to \mathcal{Y}$ is a linear operator.}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Moreover, $f$ is assumed to have coordinatewise Lipschitz continuous partial derivatives with constants $\beta_i$ and $g, h$ admit easily computable proximal operators.
elements.sent-2411-113: The iterations of PURE-CD are given by Algorithm~\ref{alg:stripd .}
elements.sent-2411-114: Given positive probabilities $(p_i)_{1 \leq i \leq n}$, the algorithm requires the notation $J(i) = \{ j\in\{1, \dots, m\}\colon M_{j, i \neq 0 \}$, $I(j) = \{ i\in\{1, \dots, n\}\colon M_{j, i \neq 0 \}$, $\pi_j = \sum_{i \in I(j) p_i$, $\underline p = \min_i p_i$, $\theta_j = \frac{\pi_j {\underline p}$ and step size parameters $\tau_i$ and $\sigma_j$ such that \begin{equation}\tau_i < \frac{2p_i - p}{\beta_i p_i + \underline p^{-1}p_i\sum_{j=1}^m \pi_j \sigma_j M_{j, i}^2}. \label{purecd_stepsizes}\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001:
\caption{Primal-dual method with random extrapolation and coordinate descent (PURE-CD)} \label{alg:stripd}  egin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$k = 0,1\ldots $} \STATE $ ar{y}_{k+1} = \prox_{\sigma, h^\ast}\left(y_k + \sigma Mx_kight)$ \STATE $ ar{x}_{k+1} = \prox_{	au, g}\left(x_k - 	au \left(

eq i_{k+1}$ \STATE $y_{k+1}^{j} =  ar{y}_{k+1}^j + \sigma_j 	heta_j(M (x_{k+1} - x_k))_j,  orall j \in J(i_{k+1})$, $y_{k+1}^j = y_k^j,  orall j
ot\in J(i_{k+1})$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-115: We numerically checked the result of \cite{alacaoglu2020random by looking for a Lyapunov function associated to the algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-116: We considered the case where $f=0$, $n = 2$, $m=1$ and $p = (1/2, 1/2)$, so that $\pi = \pi_1 = 1$ and $\theta = \theta_1 = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-117: We also fixed the condition on $M$ that $\|M_{1, i}\|^2 \leq 1$ for all $i$.
elements.sent-2411-118: This is slightly more general than PDHG and already involves randomness in the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-119: In this setting, \eqref{purecd_stepsizes simplifies into $n \tau_i \sigma \|M_{1, i}\|^2 = \gamma < 1$, $\forall i \in \{1, 2\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: We looked for a Lyapunov function involving, for $i \in \{1, 2\}$, the vectors $x_\star^i$, $y^*$, $M_{1, i x_\star^i$, $ M_{1, i ^\top y_*$, $x_0^i$, $y_0$, $M_{1, i x_0^i$, $M_{1, i ^\top y_0$, $(h^*)'(\bar y_1) \in \partial h^*(\bar y_1)$, $M_{1, i ^\top \bar y_1$, $g_i'(\bar x_1^i) \in \partial g_i(\bar x_1^i)$ and $M_{1,i \bar x_1^i$ as well as the nonnegative quantities defined by $D_P(x_0) = \sum_i g_i(x_0^i) - g_i(x_*^i) + y_\star^\top M (x_0 - x_\star)$, $D_D(y_0) = h(\bar y_1) - h(y_\star) - (\bar y_1 - y_\star)^\top M x_\star$, $\mathbb E[\|y_1 - y_\star\|^2]$ and $\mathbb E[\|x_1^i - x_\star^i\|^2]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Moreover, we consider the expected difference between successive iterates as the measure of convergence $R(z_0, z_\star) = \mathbb E[ \sum_{i=1 ^n \frac 1{\tau_i \|x_0^i - x_1^i\|^2 + \frac{1 {\sigma \|y_0 - y_1\|^2]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: After compilation with PEPit and DSP-CVXPY, we obtain two semi-definite programs, each with 7 SDP constraints involving 1409 variables, 63 affine inequality constraints and more that 1000 equality constraints.
elements.sent-2411-123: These are then sent to the SCS solver~\cite{odonoghue:21 for numerical resolution.}
elements.sent-2411-124: For $\gamma =1.1$, which is beyond what is proved in \cite{alacaoglu2020random , we get \begin{equation}\min_{\{V \in \mathcal Q\}} \max_{\{(f, g, L) \in \mathcal F, z \in \mathcal Z, z^*  saddle point\}} \mathbb E[V(PURE-CD(f,g,L, z))] + R(z) - V(z) \approx 0.5 > 0\end{equation} This shows that there does not exist a Lyapunov function for this algorithm which is a combination of quadratic terms in the vectors listed above and conic combination of the nonnegative quantities listed above.}

elements.sent-2411-125: To get a more precise answer, we should either find a counter-example where the algorithm diverges or a Lyapunov function that will necessarily be more general.

elements.sent-2411-127: In theory, since this is what is proved in \cite{alacaoglu2020random , we could also obtain a more general Lyapunov function that does not require that $z_\star$ should be a saddle point.}
elements.sent-2411-128: However, except for the case $n=1$, the solver struggles to solve the problem and terminates with an inaccurate solution.
elements.sent-2411-129: In this paper, we showed how to combine several recent works on performance estimation problems and numerical solvers in order to discover automatically Lyapunov functions for optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-130: We then showed how this technique can be used to make discoveries on the properties of complex optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-131: Firstly, we studied the linear convergence of primal-dual hybrid gradient under quadratic error bound of the smooth gap with a larger step size range than previous works.
elements.sent-2411-132: Secondly, we could conjecture and prove a slightly improved convergence rate result for randomized coordinate descent.
elements.sent-2411-133: Thirdly, we experienced the limits of the approach when studying a randomized primal-dual coordinate update method.
elements.sent-2411-134: This work paves the way to the use of performance estimation problems to investigate complex questions.
elements.sent-2411-135: Note however, that in order to really get results, we need semi-definite programming solvers able to accurately solve large and ill-conditioned problems.
elements.sent-2411-136: In future works, we plan to use the automatic Lyapunov function method to study algorithms for nonconvex-nonconcave saddle point problems.
elements.sent-2411-137: This work was supported by the Agence National de la Recherche grant ANR-20-CE40-0027, Optimal Primal-Dual Algorithms (APDO).. \bibliographystyle{alpha.}
